Mountain areas, rough terrain, have been modelled and estimated through a simple model following [cesbio](https://www.cesbio.cnrs.fr/multitemp/2481/) (https://www.cesbio.cnrs.fr/multitemp/2481/)

The slope_eff function computes the terrain-induced relative effect. Here are the mathematical equations:

[Input Variables:]{.underline}

- $\theta_{s}$ : Solar Zenith Angle (SZA)

- $\phi_{s}$ : Solar Azimuth Angle (SAA)

- $s$ : Terrain slope (computed from DEM using xdem python package)

- $\phi_{a}$ : Terrain aspect (computed from DEM using xdem python package)

[Derived Quantities:]{.underline}

- Cosine of solar zenith angle:

$$\mu_{s} = cos\left( \theta_{s} \right)$$

- Cosine of terrain slope:

$$\mu = cos(s)$$

- Effective cosine of incidence angle on tilted surface:

$$\mu_{i} = \mu_{s} \cdot \mu + \sqrt{1 - \mu_{s}^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \mu^{2}} \cdot cos\left( \phi_{s} - \phi_{a} \right)$$

- Sky view factor:

$$f_{sky} = \frac{1 + \mu}{2}$$

- Ground view factor:

$$f_{ground} = \frac{1 - \mu}{2}$$

- Slope Error

$$slope_{eff} = \frac{T}{T_{dir} \cdot \frac{\mu_{i}}{\mu_{s}} + T_{dif} \cdot f_{sky} + T \cdot f_{ground} \cdot \rho_{env}}$$

- ${u\left( R_{toc}^{j} \right)}_{RTM - slope} = R_{toc}^{j}*(slope_{eff} - 1)$

Where:

$T$ : Total atmospheric transmittance (an output of the SMAC function)

$T_{dir}$ : Direct atmospheric transmittance (an output of the SMAC function)

$T_{dif}$ : Diffuse atmospheric transmittance (an output of the SMAC function)

$\rho_{env}:$ Average environment reflectance, (supposed here equal to pixel ground reflectance)

+--------------+-----------------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Descriptor   | Value                                   | Notes                               |
+:=============+:========================================+:====================================+
| Significance | Moderate to High in mountainous regions |                                     |
+--------------+-----------------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Maturity     | 1 - Initial assessment                  | From CESBIO simple model (see text) |
+--------------+-----------------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Magnitude    | Several %                               | Could be 40%                        |
+--------------+-----------------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Mitigation   | Simple model                            | From CESBIO simple model (see text) |
+--------------+-----------------------------------------+-------------------------------------+

Table : RTM terrain effect uncertainty summary

#### 7c. SMAC Polynomial Fitting Errors

The estimation of the uncertainty due to the SMAC fitting errors is presented in section 8.4

+---------------+---------------------------------------+-------------------------------+
| Descriptor    | Value                                 | Notes                         |
+:==============+:======================================+:==============================+
| Significance  | Moderate                              |                               |
+---------------+---------------------------------------+-------------------------------+
| Maturity      | 3 - Quantified through RT comparisons |                               |
+---------------+---------------------------------------+-------------------------------+
| Magnitude     | \~1-10% RMSE vs. 6S                   | Validated with neural network |
+---------------+---------------------------------------+-------------------------------+

Table : SMAC fitting uncertainty summary

## Uncertainty Characterization Summary

### Input Uncertainty Estimates

Following the FIDUCEO approach, all input uncertainties are documented:

+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| Input Parameter    | Source               | Uncertainty Estimate | Notes                                                     |
+:===================+:=====================+:=====================+:==========================================================+
| R~toa~             | Level 1C FDR         | From DTOA-r          | Band-dependent, separated into random/structured/common   |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| UO₃                | MERRA-2 + literature | 6%                   | Wargan et al. (2017), Davis et al. (2017)                 |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| UH₂O               | MERRA-2 + estimate   | 20%                  | Conservative, needs validation                            |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| P₀                 | MERRA-2 SLP          | \<1 hPa              | Meteorological model uncertainty                          |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| λ (lapse rate)     | Model assumption     | 0.002 K/m            | Van Besselaar et al. (2011)                               |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| z (elevation)      | GTOPO30 + geoloc     | Delev (DEM stddev)   | Convolution with σ=0.5 pixels (\~0.3 VGT pixel)           |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| τ~a~⁵⁵⁰            | MERRA-2 + validation | max(0.03, 0.15×τa)   | Gueymard & Yang (2020), regionalized                      |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| Aerosol model      | AMIP ensemble        | Ensemble spread      | 10-member AMIP runs                                       |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+
| RTM approximations | Modelling            | 1-30%                | Very scene dependent. Worst case: rough terrain, high AOD |
+--------------------+----------------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------+

### Correlation Structures

+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Effect              | Spatial Correlation   | Temporal Correlation | Implementation                      |
+:====================+:======================+:=====================+:====================================+
| TOA-r (random)      | None                  | None                 | Average as √(Σu²)/N                 |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| TOA-r (structured)  | Scan-line or column   | None                 | Use L1C correlation matrix          |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| TOA-r (common)      | Global                | Global               | Simple average (no reduction)       |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| UO₃                 | \~500 km (synoptic)   | \~3 days             | Exponential decay                   |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| UH₂O                | \~200 km (convective) | \~1 day              | Exponential decay                   |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Ps (meteorological) | Global                | \>1 month            | Common (no reduction)               |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Ps (elevation)      | DEM resolution        | Permanent            | Structured (terrain-correlated)     |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| τ~a~⁵⁵⁰             | \~200-500 km (plume)  | \~1 week             | Exponential decay                   |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Aerosol model       | 1° (climatology grid) | 1 week               | Rectangle function                  |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+
| RT approximations   | Scene-dependent       | None                 | Random or systematic (case-by-case) |
+---------------------+-----------------------+----------------------+-------------------------------------+

## Limitations and Future Improvements

### Known Limitations

Current Implementation:

- Surface BRDF: Simplified kernel model
  - Impact: Up to 10% error in directional reflectance over non-forest surfaces
- Terrain effect: Simplified model
- Water Vapor Uncertainty: Conservative 20% estimate
  - Impact: May overestimate total uncertainty in dry conditions
  - Future: Refined characterization from MERRA-2 validation studies
- Adjacency Effects: Not corrected
  - Impact: Significant for high-contrast scenes (coast, cloud edge)
  - Option: Implement semi-empirical adjacency correction

> **Status update (v1.1):** Adjacency effects remain a **confirmed known limitation** (not corrected in the operational chain). Köppen-Geiger regional AOD uncertainty multipliers (Section 5.3.5) remain a **planned feature**: the classification data (`kg_zoneNumGueymard.csv`) is available in ANCILLARY, but the regional multipliers are not yet applied in `compute_urtoc()`. See Appendix D.3 for details.

### Second-Order Effects (u²(0) term)

Effects considered minor and not explicitly propagated:

+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+
| Effect                                  | Estimated Magnitude | Justification                      |
+:========================================+:====================+:===================================+
| Trace gas absorption (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O)    | \<0.001             | Window channel placement           |
+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+
| Rayleigh scattering altitude dependence | \<0.002             | Accurate DEM + pressure correction |
+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+
| LUT interpolation errors                | \<0.001             | Dense sampling validated vs. 6S    |
+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+
| Aerosol hygroscopic growth              | \<0.005             | Implicit in fixed RH=80% model     |
+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+
| Polarization                            | \<0.003             | Minor for broad-band sensors       |
+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+
| Spectral band mismatch                  | \<0.002             | SMAC computed for actual RSR       |
+-----------------------------------------+---------------------+------------------------------------+

Table : Second order uncertainty summary

**Combined second-order**: u(0) ≈ 0.007 (added quadratically to total)

# Conclusions

This ATBD demonstrates a comprehensive implementation of the FIDUCEO uncertainty propagation methodology for atmospheric correction of satellite vegetation sensors. Key achievements:

- **Rigorous Uncertainty Quantification**: All major uncertainty sources are identified, characterized, and propagated through the measurement equation

- **Operational AOD Uncertainty Model**: Based on extensive MERRA-2 validation (Gueymard & Yang 2020), providing realistic AOD uncertainties: σ(τ~a~) = max(0.03, 0.15×τa) with regional adjustments

- **Ensemble Approach for Model Uncertainty**: AMIP 10-member ensemble quantifies aerosol optical property uncertainties

- **Efficient GPU Implementation**: SMAC-CL enables fast processing while computing full Jacobian matrices

- **Validation Framework**: Following Aerosol_cci and FIDUCEO best practices (Popp & Mittaz 2022)

This approach provides climate-quality TOC reflectances with fully traceable, metrologically sound uncertainties suitable for:

- Long-term surface monitoring

- Multi-sensor harmonization

- Climate model validation

# References

**FIDUCEO Methodology:**

\- Mittaz, J., Merchant, C.J., Woolliams, E.R. (2019). Applying principles of metrology to historical Earth observations from satellites. *Metrologia*, 56, 032002.

\- Popp, T., Mittaz, J. (2022). Systematic Propagation of AVHRR AOD Uncertainties---A Case Study to Demonstrate the FIDUCEO Approach. *Remote Sensing*, 14(4), 875.

**MERRA-2 Validation:**

\- Gueymard, C., Yang, D. (2020). Worldwide validation of CAMS and MERRA-2 reanalysis aerosol optical depth products using 15 years of AERONET observations. *Atmospheric Environment*, 225, 117216.

\- Wargan, K., et al. (2017). Evaluation of the Ozone Fields in NASA's MERRA-2 Reanalysis. *Journal of Climate*, 30, 2961-2988.

\- Davis, S.M., et al. (2017). Assessment of upper tropospheric and stratospheric water vapor and ozone in reanalyses. *Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics*, 17, 12743-12778.

**Atmospheric Correction:**

\- Rahman, H., Dedieu, G. (1994). SMAC: A simplified method for the atmospheric correction of satellite measurements in the solar spectrum. *International Journal of Remote Sensing*, 15, 123-143.

\- Vermote, E.F., et al. (1997). Second Simulation of the Satellite Signal in the Solar Spectrum (6S). *IEEE TGRS*, 35, 675-686.

**Aerosol Climatology:**

\- Kinne, S., et al. (2006). An AeroCom initial assessment---optical properties in aerosol component modules of global models. *Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics*, 6, 1815-1834.

**Surface Pressure:**

\- Van Besselaar, E.J.M., et al. (2011). A European daily high-resolution observational gridded data set of sea level pressure. *Journal of Geophysical Research*, 116, D11110.

\- Breon, F.-M., A. Vermeulen and J. Descloitres, An evaluation of satellite aerosol products against sunphotometer measurements, Remote Sensing of Environment, Volume **115**, Issue 12, pp 3102-3111, 2011. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2011.06.017>.

\- Buchard, V., C.A. Randles, A.M. da Silva, A. Darmenov, P.R. Colarco, R. Govindaraju, R. Ferrare, J. Hair, A.J. Beyersdorf, L.D. Ziemba, and H. Yu, The MERRA-2 Aerosol Reanalysis, 1980 Onward. Part II: Evaluation and Case Studies. J. Climate, **30**, 6851--6872, 2017. <https://doi.org/10.1175/JCLI-D-16-0613.1>

\- Ramon D. and R. Santer, Operational remote sensing of aerosols over land to account for directional effects, Applied Optics-LP, **40**, 3060-3075 (2001).

\- Randles, C.A., A. M. da Silva, V. Buchard, P. R. Colarco, A. Darmenov, R. Govindaraju, A. Smirnov, B. Holben, R. Ferrare, J. Hair, Y. Shinozuka, and C. J. Flynn, The MERRA-2 Aerosol Reanalysis, 1980 Onward. Part I: System Description and Data Assimilation Evaluation. Journal of Climate **30**:17, 6823-6850, 2017.

Appendices

# Appendix A: SMAC Coefficient Calculation

## Spectral response functions and gaseous absorption

We give here typical atmospheric gaseous transmissions in the Proba-V spectral bands and the RSRF for the CENTER camera. All radiative transfer computations have also been done for LEFT and RIGHT cameras RSRF's

![](./media/media/image7.png){width="4.803149606299213in" height="3.2007874015748032in"}![](./media/media/image8.png){width="4.803149606299213in" height="3.2007874015748032in"}![](./media/media/image9.png){width="4.803149606299213in" height="3.2007874015748032in"}![](./media/media/image10.png){width="4.803149606299213in" height="3.2007874015748032in"}

## Processing chain

In Figure 5 is shown how SMAC coefficients are derived from basic data.

![](./media/media/image11.png){width="6.15625in" height="3.3991786964129482in"}

Figure 5 Organization of the radiative transfer tools at HYGEOS for computing atmospheric Look Up Tables from primary data

## Gaseous absorption modelling

With the RSRF data as described in the previous section, we computed the typical absorption based on the spectroscopic HITRAN 2012/2016 database (Gordon et al., 2017) and the line by line radiative transfer tools Py4Cats (Schreier et al., 2013) and/or HAPI (Kochanov et al., 2016). Then regression of gaseous transmission versus gas concentrations, air masses and barometric pressure (see Figure 6) were computed to yield SMAC coefficients fitting the gaseous absorption. Correlated K-distribution coefficients computed with the new tool PyKdis, developed by HYGEOS, are also obtained in this step, for further use in the simulation of real TOA radiances for both sensors.

![](./media/media/image12.png){width="5.118055555555555in" height="3.8381944444444445in"}

Figure 6: Example of the fit of water vapor transmission in band 2 of Proba-V Center camera. SMAC fitting model is T = exp ( a . (m. U~H2O~)^n^), where m is the air mass and U~H2O~ is the water vapor column

## Radiative transfer computations and fit accuracy

Radiative transfer equation for each pixel (geometry, band, AOT, P~surf~) is solved independently using ARTDECO RT package (<http://www.icare.univ-lille1.fr/projects/artdeco>). The TOA reflectance computations are done for a black surface and no gaseous absorption to simulate scattering quantities used in 6S/SMAC, i.e.:

- TOA reflectance (ρ^toa^) for aerosols and Rayleigh mixing, Aerosols and Rayleigh only

- Total (direct + diffuse) Transmission

- Spherical albedo

For each band, those optical quantities are simulated for various geometries, surface pressure, aerosol optical thicknesses... For example, for the TOA reflectance:

- RAA: 121 values from 0 to 180°

- SZA : 38 values from 0 to 89° (but used only if \<70°)

- VZA: 47 values from 0 to 89° (but used only if \<70°)

- Surface pressure: 6 values from 600 to 1050 hPa

- AOT at 550 nm: 16 values from 0.01 to 1.5 (but used only if \< 0.8)

Several multi-dimensional fits are performed to give the SMAC model parameters of scattering quantities. An estimation of the quality of the fit is also obtained, giving the potential to estimate the radiative transfer modeling error (see Figure 7, Figure 8, Figure 9).

![](./media/media/image13.png){width="4.803149606299213in" height="3.2007874015748032in"}

Figure 7: Example of accuracy of the SMAC model compared to ARTDECO. Aerosol TOA reflectance for band 01 of Proba-V center camera for one SZA and VZA and for all azimuth, for AOT at 550 nm ranging from 0.01 to 1 and for the aerosol model #1. The fit was performed for all VZA \< 60, SZA \<70, AOT (550) \<0.76, all azimuths. The accuracy decreases above an AOT at 550 nm of \~ 0.5

![](./media/media/image14.png){width="4.803149606299213in" height="3.2007874015748032in"}

Figure 8 : Same as Figure 7 but for TOA atmospheric reflectance.

![](./media/media/image15.png){width="6.3in" height="3.1180555555555554in"}

Figure : Rtoa SMAC Fit mean accuracy as a function of the various inputs. Statistics are given for all the LUT nodes and AOT (550) \<0.76.

### References

Schreier, F., Gimeno García, S.; Hochstaffl, P., Städt, S., (2019) Py4CAtS---PYthon for Computational ATmospheric Spectroscopy. *Atmosphere*, ***10***, 262. https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos10050262

I. E. Gordon, L. S. Rothman, C. Hill, R. V. Kochanov, Y. Tan, *et al.*, (2017) The HITRAN2016 molecular spectroscopic database, *J. Quant. Spectrosc. Radiat. Transfer* **203**, 3-69 (2017)

R.V. Kochanov, I.E. Gordon, L.S. Rothman, P. Wcislo, C. Hill, J.S. Wilzewski (2016). HITRAN Application Programming Interface (HAPI): A comprehensive approach to working with spectroscopic data, *J. Quant. Spectrosc. Radiat. Transfer,* **177**, 15-30 (2016).

<https://hitran.org/hapi>

Hess, M.; Koepke, P. & Schult, I, (1998). Optical Properties of Aerosols and Clouds: The Software Package OPAC. *Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society*, 1998, **79**, 831-844

### Fit Uncertainty Model

This part aims to estimate the incertitude of the outputted reflectance of SMAC using lightweight and fast Neural Network. Those models will be exclusively Feed Forward Neural Network (also called MLP) and will be small to satisfy hardware constraint on memory usage.

The required library for the inference of ML model is ONNXRuntime. It is a library that optimizes and executes machine learning models in ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) format. In operational workflows, it enables fast, efficient model deployment by opening model as black-box function.

[Inputs]{.underline} :

- Angles (SZA, VZA, RAA)

- AOD at 550 nm

- Pression

- TOC-r

- iaer

- TOA-r

After using the SMAC coefficients simulations representing more than 106 points, we performed a test using a very fast pots processing pure python NN inference for one band. The test outcome, shown hereafter, is promising to show a Mean Square Error of 0.0097 on the predicted RTM uncertainty on R~toc~

> ![](./media/media/image16.png){width="6.3in" height="2.3506944444444446in"}

## BRDF effect estimation

The baseline AC are run under the assumption of a lambertian surface. If the shape of the surface BRDF is known, we can optionally include this knowledge into the AC scheme with the following modelling

### Surface BRDF model (RossThick-LiSparse-R)

In the following, we restrict the description to a model with three parameters of the form:

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                             |
+:============================================================================================+
| $\rho_{g}$*(θ~s~, θ~v~, ϕ) = k~0~ + k~1~F~1~(θ~s~, θ~v~, ϕ) + k~2~F~2~(θ~s~, θ~v~, ϕ)* (25) |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

While *k~0~* quantifies an isotropic contribution to the reflectance factor *F~0~*=1, functions *F~1~* and *F~2~* represent the angular distribution related to geometric and volumetric surface scattering processes, respectively. Lucht et al. (2000) suggest the RossThick-LiSparse-Reciprocal model (simply referred to as the Ross-Li BRDF model) that results from the combination of two models for the definition of the volumetric (RossThick) and geometric (LiSparse-R) kernels. The geometric kernel is expressed as follows:

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
+:=======================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================+
| $$F_{1}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right) = O\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right) - sec\left( {\theta\ '}_{s} \right) - sec\left( {\theta\ }_{v}^{'} \right) + \frac{1}{2}\left( 1 + cos(\xi\ ') \right)\sec\left( {\theta\ '}_{s} \right)\sec\left( {\theta\ }_{v}^{'} \right)$$ (26) |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where,

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------+
| $$O = \frac{1}{\pi}\left( t - sin(t)\cos(t) \right)\left( \sec\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right) - sec\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right) \right)$$                                                                         | \(27\)  |
+:=====================================================================================================================================================================================================================+:========+
| $$\cos{(t) = 2}\frac{\sqrt{D^{2} + \left( \tan\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right)\tan\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right)\sin(\phi) \right)^{2}}}{\sec\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right) + sec\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right)}$$ | \(28\)  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------+
| $$D = \sqrt{\tan^{2}\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right) + \tan^{2}\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right) - 2tan\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right)\tan\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right)\cos(\phi)}$$                                  | \(29\)  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------+
| $$\cos\left( \xi^{\ '} \right) = cos\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right)\cos\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right) + sin\left( \theta_{s}^{\ '} \right)\sin\left( \theta_{v}^{\ '} \right)\cos(\phi)$$                           | \(30\)  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------+
| $\theta_{s}^{\ '} = \tan^{- 1}\left( \frac{b_{RL}}{r}\tan\theta_{s} \right)$ and $\theta_{v}^{\ '} = \tan^{- 1}\left( \frac{b_{RL}}{r}\tan\theta_{v} \right)$                                                        | \(31\)  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------+

And the volumetric kernel is defined as:

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
+:========================================================================================================================================================================================================+
| $$F_{2}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right) = \frac{\left( \frac{\pi}{2} - \xi \right)\cos(\xi) + sin(\xi)}{\cos\left( \theta_{s} \right) + cos\left( \theta_{v} \right)} - \frac{\pi}{4}$$ (32) |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                                                            |
+:===========================================================================================================================================================================================+
| $$\cos\left( \xi^{\ } \right) = cos\left( \theta_{s}^{\ } \right)\cos\left( \theta_{v}^{\ } \right) + sin\left( \theta_{s}^{\ } \right)\sin\left( \theta_{v}^{\ } \right)\cos(\phi)$$ (33) |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

The choice is used here: *h/b~RL~*=2 and *b~RL~/r*=1.

We can summarize the surface BRDF modelling as:

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-----------+
| $$\rho_{g} = k_{0}\left\lbrack 1 + k_{1}^{p}F_{1} + k_{2}^{p}F_{2} \right\rbrack$$                | \(34\)    |
|                                                                                                   |           |
| with $k_{1}^{p} = \frac{k_{1}}{k_{0}} ;\ k_{2}^{p} = \frac{k_{2}}{k_{0}}$ |           |
+===================================================================================================+===========+
|                                                                                                   |           |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-----------+

kp's are normalized coefficients that describe the departure from the lambertian reflector (in that case${\ k}_{1}^{p} = k_{2}^{p} = 0$**), this is the baseline processing**.

phic correction including BRDF effects

### Fast radiative transfer model with BRDF

In the case of a horizontally homogeneous target, the surface BRDF shape can be included through the 6S formulation (*Vermote et al., 1997*), and the atmospheric correction equations \[2-4\] are the same except that we have a modified total atmospheric transmission $T_{atm;g}$(*Ramon and Santer, 2001*):

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
+:
| $$T_{atm;g} = \left( 1 - D\left( \mu_{v} \right) \right)\left( 1 - D\left( \mu_{s} \right) \right) + \frac{\overline{\rho_{g}}}{\rho_{g}}\left( 1 - D\left( \mu_{v} \right) \right)D\left( \mu_{s} \right) + \frac{\overline{\rho_{g}}'}{\rho_{g}}D\left( \mu_{v} \right)\left( 1 - D\left( \mu_{s} \right) \right) + \frac{\overline{\overline{\rho_{g}}}}{\rho_{g}}D\left( \mu_{v} \right)D\left( \mu_{s} \right)$$ (35) |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where *D* is the ratio of diffuse over total transmission and $\ \overline{\rho_{g}}\ \ and\ \overline{\overline{\rho_{g}}}$ are single and double directional weighted averages of the surface BRDF. The weight is the atmospheric downward radiance$\ L_{atm}^{\downarrow}$ at the ground level:

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+--------+
| $$\overline{\rho_{g}} = \ \overline{\rho_{g}}(\theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi) = \ \frac{\int_{0}^{2\pi}{\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{\cos\theta^{'}L_{atm}^{\downarrow}\left( \theta_{s}{,\ \theta}^{'},\varphi^{'} \right)\rho_{g}\left( \theta^{'},\theta_{v},\varphi^{'} - \phi \right)\sin{\theta^{'}d\theta^{'}d\varphi^{'}}}}}{\int_{0}^{2\pi}{\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{\cos{\theta^{'}L_{atm}^{\downarrow}\left( \theta_{s}{,\ \theta}^{'},\varphi^{'} \right)}}}\sin{\theta^{'}d\theta^{'}d\varphi^{'}}}$$ | \(36\) |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |        |
| ${\overline{\rho_{g}}}^{'}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right)$= $\overline{\rho_{g}}(\theta_{v}{,\theta}_{s},\phi)$                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |        |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |        |
| $$\overline{\overline{\rho_{g}}} = \ \overline{\overline{\rho_{g}}}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right) = \ \overline{{\overline{\rho_{g}}}^{'}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right)}$$                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |        |

|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |        |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+--------+

The linearization of the ground reflectance allows the ground to derive depending on terms as a function of just kernel directional averages $\overline{F_{1}},\overline{F_{2}},\overline{\overline{F_{1}}},\overline{\overline{F_{2}}}$:

+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------+
| $$\overline{\rho_{g}} = k_{0}\left\lbrack 1 + k_{1}^{p}\overline{F_{1}} + k_{2}^{p}\overline{F_{2}} \right\rbrack,$$                                 | \(37\)   |
|                                                                                                                                                      |          |
| $$\overline{\overline{\rho_{g}}} = k_{0}\left\lbrack 1 + k_{1}^{p}\overline{\overline{F_{1}}} + k_{2}^{p}\overline{\overline{F_{2}}} \right\rbrack$$ |          |
+======================================================================================================================================================+==========+
|                                                                                                                                                      |          |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                             |
+:============================================================================================================================================================+
| $$\frac{\overline{\rho_{g}}}{\rho_{g}} = \ \frac{1 + k_{1}^{p}{\overline{F}}_{1} + k_{2}^{p}{\overline{F}}_{2}}{1 + k_{1}^{p}F_{1} + k_{2}^{p}F_{2}}$$ (38) |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                                |
+:===============================================================================================================================================================+
| $$\frac{\overline{\rho_{g}}'}{\rho_{g}} = \ \frac{1 + k_{1}^{p}{\overline{F}}_{1}' + k_{2}^{p}{\overline{F}}_{2}'}{1 + k_{1}^{p}F_{1} + k_{2}^{p}F_{2}}$$ (39) |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                                                                                                                                            |
+:===========================================================================================================================================================================================+
| $$\frac{\overline{\overline{\rho_{g}}}}{\rho_{g}} = \ \frac{1 + k_{1}^{p}\overline{\overline{F_{1}}} + k_{2}^{p}{\overline{\overline{F}}}_{2}}{1 + k_{1}^{p}F_{1} + k_{2}^{p}F_{2}}$$ (40) |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

The estimation of BRDF effects requires the knowledge of $k_{1}^{p}$ and${\ k}_{2}^{p}$, the kernels and their directional averages are analytically computed

SMAC general philosophy is to approximate atmospheric functions with simple and fast regression. We thus analysed the variations of the kernel directional averages as a function of wavelength and aerosol optical thickness. The computation has been made with exact radiative transfer computations for the downward atmospheric radiances and integration using Equations (38). Figure 10 and Figure 11 show some examples of $\overline{{\ F}_{1}},\overline{F_{2}},\overline{\overline{F_{1}}},\overline{\overline{F_{2}}}$ in the NIR for one aerosol model and 2 AOTs as well as the case where the sky radiance is supposed isotropic. In the latter case the kernel directional single (resp. double) integral is simply the hemispherical-directional (resp. bi hemispherical) albedo. Directionality is still pronounced for single integrals, and smooth for double ones. It is mainly dependent on wavelength and not AOT and the directional behaviour is like the kernels themselves. So the idea here is to make a computation of $\overline{{\ F}_{1}},\overline{F_{2}},\overline{\overline{F_{1}}},\overline{\overline{F_{2}}}$ as linear combinations of the kernels F~1~ and F~2~, the coefficients of the combinations being only functions of the spectral channel.

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------+
| $${\overline{F}}_{1}\  = d_{1}^{0} + \ d_{1}^{1}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}\mathbf{\ } + \ d_{1}^{2}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}$$            | \(41\)   |
|                                                                                                                                      |          |
| $${\overline{F}}_{2}\  = d_{2}^{0} + \ d_{2}^{1}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}\mathbf{\ } + \ d_{2}^{2}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}$$            |          |
|                                                                                                                                      |          |
| $$\overline{\overline{F_{1}}}\  = b_{1}^{0} + \ b_{1}^{1}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}\mathbf{\ } + \ b_{1}^{2}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}$$   |          |
|                                                                                                                                      |          |
| $${\overline{\overline{F}}}_{2}\  = b_{2}^{0} + \ b_{2}^{1}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}\mathbf{\ } + \ b_{2}^{2}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}$$ |          |
|                                                                                                                                      |          |
| $${\overline{F}}_{1}^{'} = d_{1}^{0} + \ d_{1}^{1}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}^{'} + \ d_{1}^{2}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}^{'}$$             |          |
|                                                                                                                                      |          |
| $${\overline{F}}_{2}^{'} = d_{2}^{0} + \ d_{2}^{1}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}^{'} + \ d_{2}^{2}\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}^{'}$$             |          |
+======================================================================================================================================+==========+
|                                                                                                                                      |          |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------+

Where, by convention, we noted:

+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+------------+
| $${\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}} = \ F}_{1}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right)$$  | \(42\)     |
|                                                                                     |            |
| $${\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}} = \ F}_{2}\left( \theta_{s}{,\theta}_{v},\phi \right)$$  |            |
|                                                                                     |            |
| $${\mathbf{F'}_{\mathbf{1}} = \ F}_{1}\left( \theta_{v}{,\theta}_{s},\phi \right)$$ |            |
|                                                                                     |            |
| $${\mathbf{F'}_{\mathbf{2}} = \ F}_{2}\left( \theta_{v}{,\theta}_{s},\phi \right)$$ |            |
+=====================================================================================+============+
|                                                                                     |            |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+------------+

The 12 regressions coefficients $d_{i}^{k}$ and $b_{i}^{k}$ are stored in the SMAC coefficient's structure. F~1~ and F~2~ are analytical functions, computed once per pixel. Therefore, the corrective BRDF terms are calculated extremely fast.

The accuracy of such modelling has been checked against exact computations and compared to the accuracy of the lambertian assumption. A typical example is given in Figure 12 and Figure 13 for a scene that consists of a vegetation target in the NIR with a high aerosol load, where the BRDF effects are the highest. On can see that the lambertian assumption leads to directional biases that could reach 10% for oblique views and SZA=60°. Our simple model significantly reduces the intensity and directional dependence of those biases.

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| ![](./media/media/image17.png){width="5.822834645669292in" height="2.4960629921259843in"} |
+:============================================================================================================================================================================================+
| ![](./media/media/image18.png){width="5.822834645669292in" height="2.4960629921259843in"} |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| ![](./media/media/image19.png){width="5.822834645669292in" height="2.4960629921259843in"} |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

Figure : Polar plots of F1: geometric kernel (left), single and double directional averages ${\overline{F}}_{1}$ (centre) and $\overline{\overline{F_{1}}}$ (right) as a function of view zenith angle (VZA) and relative azimuth angles (RAA) and for a SZA of 60° and λ=865 nm. Transects correspond to RAA=0 (black), 60 (red) and 90° (green). (TOP) AOT=0.01, (MIDDLE) AOT=0.4, (BOTTOM) the sky downward radiance is assumed isotropic.

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| ![](./media/media/image20.png){width="5.822834645669292in" height="2.4960629921259843in"} |
+:=============================================================================================================================================================================================+
| ![](./media/media/image21.png){width="5.822834645669292in" height="2.4960629921259843in"}  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| ![](./media/media/image22.png){width="5.822834645669292in" height="2.4960629921259843in"}  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

Figure : Same as Figure 10 but for the volumetric kernel${\ F}_{2}$.

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| ![](./media/media/image23.png){width="6.792361111111111in" height="2.263888888888889in"} |
+:===========================================================================================================================================================================================+
| ![](./media/media/image24.png){width="6.792361111111111in" height="2.263888888888889in"} |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

Figure 12 : Polar plots of the TOA reflectance for the Proba-V NIR band and for a typical vegetation target modelled as \[k~0~=0.36, k~1~=0.03, k~2~ =0.24\] and for SZA = 30° and AOT=0.4. The transects correspond to RAA=0° (black), and 90° (red) (Left) Exact computation, (centre) Approximation and (right) relative difference in % between approximation and exact computations. (TOP) 6S formulation where the surface is considered lambertian, (BOTTOM) 6S formulation with a modelled surface BRDF as described in this section.

| ![](./media/media/image25.png){width="6.792361111111111in" height="2.263888888888889in"} |
|:---|
| ![](./media/media/image26.png){width="6.792361111111111in" height="2.263888888888889in"} |

Figure : same as Figure 12 but for SZA=60°.

# Appendix B: Aerosol Model Definitions

## Aerosol variability on Earth

44 688 MERRA-2 grid points have been selected over land. Four dates have been chosen to represent the seasonal variability: 2011/01/01, 2011/04/01, 2011/07/01, and 2011/10/01. Data is taken at 13:00.

Global average of AOT is computed for each component for the four dates. The total AOT increases from winter to summer, especially because of desert dust and OC. Indeed, desert dust AOT increases from 0.04 to 0.10 and OC AOT from 0.02 to 0.05. The three other components AOT vary little with the season.

The aerosol models are defined according to the MERRA-2 5 AOT ratios of each aerosol component u {X~u~, u$\  \in \{ DU,SU,OC,BC,SS\ \}$}:

X~u~ = $\frac{\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}\mathbf{;}\mathbf{u}}^{\mathbf{550}}}{\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}}$

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
![](./media/media/image27.emf){width="6.075in" height="7.541666666666667in"}
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
*(Figure omitted in PDF export: original table is a Windows EMF vector graphic not convertible in this environment; see the DOCX version of this ATBD for the figure.)*
:::

Table : Statistics about AOT's of each aerosol component from MERRA-2 analysis of 4 days in 2011

Each mixing ratio X~u~ covers the 0-100% range except BC which remains smaller than 25% on the four dates. The SU, DU, OC and SS X~u~ are defined between 0 and 100% with steps of 20%, and the BC X~u~ is defined between 0 and 30% with a step of 5%.

From the 5x5x5x5x6=3750 possible combinations, 148 combinations of the component X~u~ effectively occur on the 44 688 pixels of 2011/07/01. These 148 combinations of the components X~u~ define 148 aerosol models. (See Figure 14)

![](./media/media/image28.png){width="6.146489501312336in" height="3.7656889763779526in"}

Figure : AOT's ratio's X~u~ of the 5 MERRA-2 aerosol components of the 148 aerosol model used in this study

## Aerosol optical properties

To calculate easily the optical properties of these mixing, we directly associate one OPAC aerosol model/component with each component u from MERRA-2 (see Table 13). For the moment the relative humidity is considered fixed to 80%

Table : Mapping between MERRA-2 aerosol component and OPAC for the computation of optical properties

| **MERRA-2 aerosol component** | **OPAC model or component (RH=80%)** |
|:------------------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| SU/SU                         | Antarctic model                      |
| DU/DU                         | Desert model                         |
| OC/OM                         | Water soluble (waso) component       |
| SS/SS                         | Maritime clean model                 |
| BC/BC                         | Soot component                       |

The aerosol optical properties necessary for SMAC are then computed like this:

Spectral dependence of the AOT K (λ)=$\frac{\tau(\lambda)}{\tau}(550)$:

$K(\lambda) = \sum_{u}^{}{K_{u}(\lambda)X}_{u}$

Single scattering albedo:

$\varpi_{0}(\lambda) = \sum_{u}^{}{\varpi_{0}^{u}{(\lambda)X}_{u}}$

Phase function:

$P(\theta,\lambda) = \sum_{u}^{}{P_{u}(\theta,\lambda)X_{u}}$

And the derived asymmetry parameter:

$g(\lambda) = \frac{1}{2}\int_{- 1}^{1}{\mu P(\mu,\lambda)}d\mu$ with $\mu = cos(\theta)$

These aerosol optical properties are plotted in Figure 15 and Figure 16.

| ![](./media/media/image29.png){width="3.5984251968503935in" height="2.3976377952755907in"} |
|:---|
| ![](./media/media/image30.png){width="3.5984251968503935in" height="2.3976377952755907in"} |
| ![](./media/media/image31.png){width="3.5984251968503935in" height="2.3976377952755907in"} |

Figure : OPAC Aerosols components optical properties with RH=80% at the Proba-V spectral bands

| ![](./media/media/image32.png){width="3.5984251968503935in" height="2.3976377952755907in"} |
|:---|
| ![](./media/media/image33.png){width="3.5984251968503935in" height="2.3976377952755907in"} |
| ![](./media/media/image34.png){width="3.5984251968503935in" height="2.3976377952755907in"} |

Figure : 148 Aerosols mixtures optical properties at the Proba-V spectral bands.

# Appendix C: SMAC-CL Implementation

## Input reading

The elevation map and its uncertainty (elev and Delev) are read from the DEM auxiliary file [once]{.underline} ( see section 2.2).

The image inputs are read from Level 1 files (Section 2.1). For each pixel of the image, the following quantities are read:

- The TOA radiance for each sensor channel *λ*: TOA-r(*λ*)

- The corresponding error: DTOA-r(*λ*),

- The corresponding solar extra-terrestrial irradiance Es(*λ*)

- The sun zenith angle (SZA), the sun azimuth angle (SAA), the view zenith angles (VZA, VZA_SWIR), and the viewing azimuth angle (VAA, VAA_SWIR),

- The latitude and longitude: lat, lon*,*

The [name of the sensor, date and mean time of acquisition (in UTC)]{.underline} should be written in the file as an [attribute]{.underline}. Indeed, the name of the sensor and the date of acquisition allow reading the right SMAC coefficients coeffs while the date allows reading the daily MERRA-2 auxiliary files as described in Section 2.3 and monthly AMIP auxiliary files as described in Section 2.4.

If the BRDF effects estimation is activated in the configuration, then we build the right name for auxiliary C3S Surface Albedo product as in section 2.6 .

## Pixel masking

The cloud contaminated are masked and not further processed. The selection is based on the cloud flag already present in the input file. The idea is to keep [the clearest pixels only]{.underline}. It is done by selecting pixels for which the flag is 0. We mask out also pixels for which SZA is above 90°.

## Spatial and temporal interpolation of auxiliary data

From elev, Delev, lat, lon, the pixel altitude **z** and its uncertainty **∆z** is obtained through a [nearest neighbor interpolation]{.underline}.

The sea-level pressure **P~0~**, the temperature above ground **T**, the total ozone column$\ \mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{3}}}$, the total column water vapor$\ \mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}\mathbf{O}}$ and the AOT at 550 nm $\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}$ , $\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;DU}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;SU}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;OC}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;BC}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;SS}}^{\mathbf{550}}$are obtained respectively from the SLP, T10M, TO3, TQV and TOTEXTTAU, DUEXTTAU, SUEXTTAU, OCEXTTAU, BCEXTTAU, SSEXTTAU MERRA-2 auxiliary data (see section2.3) through a [spatial and temporal linear interpolation]{.underline}

Then the monthly means of aerosol optical thicknesses coming from the AMIP 10 runs are loaded and [spatially interpolated]{.underline} to give $\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;AMIPj}}^{\mathbf{550}}$ ,

$\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;DU;AMIPj}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;SU;AMIPj}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;OC;AMIPj}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;BC;AMIPj}}^{\mathbf{550}}\mathbf{,}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a;SS;AMIPj}}^{\mathbf{550}}$ for **model run number j**. The fields TOTEXTTAU, DUEXTTAU, SUEXTTAU, OCEXTTAU, BCEXTTAU, SSEXTTAU from the AMIP auxiliary files are used (see section 2.4**Erreur ! Source du renvoi introuvable.**)

The auxiliary data is then converted from MERRA-2 to SMAC units:

1.  Table: MERRA-2 and SMAC units

| Variable | MERRA-2 unit | SMAC unit | Conversion factor |
|:---------|:-------------|:----------|:------------------|
| U~O3~    | Dobson       | cm.atm    | 1e-3              |
| U~H2O~   | kg.m^-2^     | g.cm^-2^  | 1e-1              |
| P~0~     | Pa           | hPa       | 1e-2              |

Then, the surface pressure **P~s~** and its uncertainty **∆P~s~** are computed using Eq. (20), (21), and (22), (23), (24).

Interpolate spatially to have BRDF parameters k~1p~ and k~2p~

## Aerosols models selections

Select the aerosol model number (between 148 models), by minimizing the distance in a 5-dimensional space corresponding to the 5 AOT ratios {X~u~, u$\  \in \{ DU,SU,OC,BC,SS\ \}$} of the 5 aerosol components

X~u~ = $\frac{\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}\mathbf{;}\mathbf{u}}^{\mathbf{550}}}{\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}}$

**iaero** = $\text{argmin}_i \sum_{u} \left(X_u - X_{u,i}\right)^{2}$, for $i=0,\ldots,147$.

The X~u,i~ basis for the 148 predefined models are stored in a text file and read once.

An example of interpolated inputs is given in Figure 17 : Example of inputs ancillary atmospheric data for a VGT sub-image

The list of aerosol models of AMIP are also computed using the same approach to give **iaero~AMIPj~** for j in 1...10

## SMAC-CL function

**Architecture:**

- Language: OpenCL (compatible with GPU and CPU)
- Interface: Python wrapper
- Processing mode: Pixel-parallel (massively parallel on GPU)

**Main Processing Function:**

```         
def smac_cl_run(
    coeffs,           # SMAC coefficients (148 aerosol models)
    tetas, tetav,     # SZA, VZA arrays
    phis, phiv,       # SAA, VAA arrays
    uh2o, uo3,        # Water vapor, ozone columns
    taup550,          # AOD at 550 nm
    pressure,         # Surface pressure
    rtoa,             # TOA reflectance
    k1p, k2p,         # BRDF kernel ratios (default 0 for Lambertian)
    iaero_list        # List of aerosol model indices (11 models)
):
    """
    Inputs: All arrays dimensioned [npixels] or [nbands, npixels]
    Outputs:
      - rtoc: TOC reflectance [nbands, npixels]
      - jacobians: Sensitivity matrices
      - u_ensemble: Std dev from aerosol ensemble
    """
```

**Processing Flow:**

```         
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. Reorganize input arrays for GPU efficiency      │
│     (transpose, flatten, pad)                       │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  2. Upload data to GPU/CPU memory                   │
│     - TOA reflectances                              │
│     - Geometry arrays                               │
│     - Auxiliary data (UH₂O, UO₃, Ps, τa)            │
│     - SMAC coefficients (pre-loaded once)           │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  3. GPU/CPU kernel execution (per-pixel parallel)   │
│     For each pixel in parallel:                     │
│       a. Compute gaseous transmissions              │
│       b. Compute Rayleigh scattering                │
│       c. Compute aerosol scattering                 │
│       d. Apply SMAC equations → Rtoc                │
│       e. Compute analytical Jacobians               │
│       f. Repeat for finite-difference Jacobians     │
│       g. Repeat for 10 aerosol ensemble members     │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  4. Download results from GPU/CPU                   │
│     - Rtoc arrays                                   │
│     - Jacobian arrays                               │
│     - Ensemble statistics                           │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  5. CPU post-processing                             │
│     - Combine uncertainty components                │
│     - Apply terrain corrections                     │
│     - Quality flag assignment                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

![](./media/media/image35.png){width="6.873611111111111in" height="3.8402777777777777in"}

**Performance:**

- GPU acceleration: \~100× faster than CPU for large images
- Memory requirement: \~2 GB GPU RAM for full-swath processing
- Typical processing time: \<TBD second per tile (1000×1000 pixels)

The SMAC-CL run procedure inputs correspond to the following quantities described in this document:

coeffs, SZA, VZA, SAA, VAA,$\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}\mathbf{O}}$,$\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{3}}}$,$\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}$, **P~s~**, TOA-r(*λ*), k1p, k2p, iaero_list.

The run method is called twice, one for the VIS-NIR bands and one for the SWIR band. iaero_list contains 11 aerosol model number. The first one is iaero, the 10 others are iaero_AMIPj ( see section 10.4).

Upon selection in the configuration, the k1p and k2p arrays are set to zero, that means that the surface is considered lambertian. A call with these coefficients not set to zero, for example using L3 BRDF products allows to estimate the effect of BRDF on the TOC-r

All the image inputs are multidimensional

The outputs of the SMAC-CL run routine are:

- The AC corrected directional reflectance $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}$ for each sensor channel *λ*

- The Jacobians$\ \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toa}}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{3}}}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}\mathbf{O}}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}}}\mathbf{\ ,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}}$ for each sensor channel *λ*

- The standard deviation ${\mathbf{u}\left( \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}} \right)}_{\mathbf{ensemble}}$ due to aerosol models spread for each sensor channel *λ*

An example of the outputs and Jacobians is given in Figure 18

## Level 2A Output Variables

The standard outputs of AC module are:

- The AC corrected directional reflectance $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}$ for each sensor channel *λ*,

- The associated uncertainties $\mathbf{\mathrm{\Delta}}\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}$for each sensor channel *λ*,

- Flags

  - Bit 0 : cloud \_contaminated (clm != 0)

  - Bit 1 : High AOD (TOA \>= 0.6)

  - Bit 2 : High SZA (sza \> 80.)

  - Bit 3 : High AOD gradient (aod_grad \> Threshold)

  - Bit 4 : AC algorithm failure

  - Bit 5 : Missing auxiliary data

  - Bit 6 : Out of LUT range

TOC directional reflectances and Jacobians are direct outputs of the SMAC procedure (done in the GPU) while the [associated uncertainties are post-processed in the CPU]{.underline} as described in section 5.2. The quantities used are:

- The Jacobians $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toa}}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{3}}}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}\mathbf{O}}}\mathbf{,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}}}\mathbf{\ ,\ }\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}}}^{\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}}$

- DTOA-r(*λ*), $\mathrm{\Delta}\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{3}}}$, $\mathrm{\Delta}\mathbf{U}_{\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}\mathbf{O}}$, $\mathrm{\Delta}\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}$, $\mathrm{\Delta}\mathbf{\tau}_{\mathbf{a}}^{\mathbf{550}}$)

- ${\mathbf{u}\left( \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}} \right)}_{\mathbf{ensemble}}$**, slope_error,** ${\mathbf{u}\left( \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{toc}} \right)}_{\mathbf{RTM}}$

The projection and the attributes of the input TOA reflectances are kept. Nevertheless, the longitude and latitude fields are also written in the output file for simplicity.

An example of the outputs is given in.

**File:** PROBAV_L1C_20141111_184634_2_1KM_V301_toc.nc

**Number of variables:** 83

## Output file structure

| **Variable** | **Dimensions** | **Shape** | **Dtype** | **Units** | **Long Name** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| AOD_550_used | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Aerosol Optical Depth at 550nm |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_AOD_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_AOD_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_AOD_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_AOD_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Ps_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | hPa-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to pressure |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Ps_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | hPa-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to pressure |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Ps_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | hPa-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to pressure |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Ps_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | hPa-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to pressure |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Rtoa_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to TOA reflectance |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Rtoa_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to TOA reflectance |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Rtoa_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to TOA reflectance |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_Rtoa_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to TOA reflectance |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UH2O_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to water vapor |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UH2O_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to water vapor |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UH2O_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to water vapor |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UH2O_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to water vapor |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UO3_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to ozone |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UO3_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to ozone |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UO3_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to ozone |
| Jacobian_Rtoc_vs_UO3_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | cm2 g-1 | Jacobian of TOC reflectance with respect to ozone |
| Quality_flag | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | int16 | N/A | N/A |
| Rtoc_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Bidirectional Reflectance Factor |
| Rtoc_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Bidirectional Reflectance Factor |
| Rtoc_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Bidirectional Reflectance Factor |
| Rtoc_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Bidirectional Reflectance Factor |
| Rtoc_uncertainty_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Reflectance Uncertainty |
| Rtoc_uncertainty_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Reflectance Uncertainty |
| Rtoc_uncertainty_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Reflectance Uncertainty |
| Rtoc_uncertainty_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | 1 | Top of Canopy Reflectance Uncertainty |
| SAA | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degree | Solar Azimuth Angle |
| SM_MAP_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | uint8 | 1 | Status Map |
| SM_MAP_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | uint8 | 1 | Status Map |
| SM_MAP_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | uint8 | 1 | Status Map |
| SM_MAP_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | uint8 | 1 | Status Map |
| SZA | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degree | Solar Zenith Angle |
| VAA | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degree | Sensor Azimuth Angle (VNIR) |
| VAA_IR | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degree | Sensor Azimuth Angle (SWIR) |
| VZA | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degree | Sensor Zenith Angle (VNIR) |
| VZA_IR | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degree | Sensor Zenith Angle (SWIR) |
| aerosol_model_index | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | int32 | N/A | N/A |
| clm | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | uint8 | 1 | Cloud Mask |
| lat | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degrees_north | Latitude |
| lon | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | degrees_east | Longitude |
| ozone_column_used | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | DU | Total Column Ozone |
| surface_pressure_used | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | Pa | Sea Level Pressure |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_BRDF_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_BRDF_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_BRDF_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_BRDF_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_fit_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_fit_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_fit_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_fit_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_terrain_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_terrain_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_terrain_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_RTM_terrain_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_TOA_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_TOA_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_TOA_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_TOA_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aerosol_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aerosol_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aerosol_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aerosol_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aod_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aod_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aod_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_aod_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_h2o_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_h2o_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_h2o_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_h2o_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_ozone_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_ozone_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_ozone_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_ozone_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_pressure_B1 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_pressure_B2 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_pressure_B3 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| uncertainty_from_pressure_B4 | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | N/A | N/A |
| water_vapor_column_used | ('height', 'width') | (4275, 2601) | float32 | kg m-2 | Total Precipitable Water Vapor |

Standard outputs variables (TOC-reflectances and total associated uncertainties). Optional outputs variables (Jacobians and uncertainty contributors). Debug output variables (MERRA-2 inputs and aerosol model index)

**Quality Flags:**

- Bit 0: Cloud-contaminated pixel
- Bit 1: High AOD (\>0.8)
- Bit 2: High SZA (\>70°)
- Bit 3: High AOD gradient (AOD quality risks)
- Bit 4: AC algorithm failure
- Bit 5: Missing auxiliary data
- Bit 6: Out-of-LUT range

**Global Attributes:**

- Processing algorithm version
- SMAC coefficients version
- MERRA-2 data version and date
- AMIP ensemble run IDs
- DEM version
- Processing date/time
- Geographic coverage

![](./media/media/image36.png){width="6.3in" height="3.0898490813648296in"}

Figure : Example of inputs ancillary atmospheric data for a VGT sub-image

![](./media/media/image37.png){width="6.297829177602799in" height="9.484722222222222in"}

Figure : Example of outputs (Jacobians and TOC reflectances)

![](./media/media/image38.png){width="4.8125in" height="10.08380905511811in"}

Figure Outputs uncertainties

![](./media/media/image39.png){width="6.3in" height="2.2256944444444446in"}

Figure Outputs flags and AOD\@550 used

# Appendix D: Implementation Notes (NEW in v1.1)

This appendix documents implementation-specific details, performance optimizations, and known limitations of the FDR4VGT production codebase (`fdr4vgt_opencl` repository). These are not changes to the scientific algorithm described in Sections 1-10, but engineering decisions for operational efficiency, plus the status of two items flagged in Section 5.5 (Known Limitations).

## D.1 Performance Optimizations

### Ancillary Zarr Cache

| Aspect | Description |
|----|----|
| What | Pre-interpolates MERRA-2 fields to the full satellite grid once, stores them in a local Zarr cache |
| Why | MERRA-2 source files sit on network storage; per-tile interpolation would re-read the network and repeat work |
| Function | `precompute_ancillary()` in `process.py` |
| Toggle | `[Sizes] anc_cache` (default on) |
| Numerical equivalence | Pointwise interpolation → identical to per-tile interpolation (tiles read from local cache with halo) |
| Memory | Streamed tile-by-tile to bound peak memory (\~2.8 GB, safe on 4 GB nodes) |

: Ancillary Zarr cache optimization

Cache format: Zarr format 2. Most fields are stored as float16 (small values, sufficient precision); `SLP` and `T10M` are stored as float32 (see D.2, overflow protection).

### Tile-Based Processing

| Aspect | Description |
|----|----|
| What | Processes the image in overlapping tiles (`batch_size` × `batch_size` with a halo) |
| Why | Bounded memory per tile, parallel processing across workers |
| Function | `process_batched()` generates the tile grid; `_worker_compute_tile()` processes each tile |
| Config | `[Sizes] chunks_size` (default 512), `[Sizes] nworkers` (default 1) |
| Halo | ±1 pixel halo for terrain slope/aspect computation (edge effects) |

: Tile-based processing optimization

### Local Output File

| Aspect | Description |
|----|----|
| What | Writes the output NetCDF to a local temporary file, then moves it to the final destination once at the end |
| Why | Serial per-tile append writes degrade badly on network storage (Ceph): \~2 s → \~5 s per tile |
| Function | `process_batched()` creates a temp file, `shutil.move()` at the end |
| Toggle | `[Sizes] local_output` (default on) |

: Local output file optimization

### Single NetCDF Handle

+----------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Aspect                           | Description                                                               |
+==================================+===========================================================================+
| What                             | Opens the output NetCDF handle once and reuses it for all tiles           |
+----------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Why                              | Per-tile open/close/flush was the dominant writer cost (HDF5 overhead)    |
+----------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Function                         | `nc_out = Dataset(output_path, 'a')` reused across tiles                  |
+----------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Flush                            | Periodic close+reopen every `flush_interval` tiles to free the HDF5 cache |
+----------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: Single NetCDF handle optimization

### DEM Cropping

| Aspect | Description |
|----|----|
| What | Crops the global GTOPO30 DEM to the product's lat/lon extent before loading |
